concepts.name: core definition
concepts.description: Deadlock is a state where a set of processes waits forever because each process is waiting for a resource held by another process in the same set.

concepts.name: key components
concepts.description: The four Coffman conditions are mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait.

concepts.name: working mechanism
concepts.description: Deadlock happens when resources are allocated in a way that forms a cyclic dependency graph among processes.

concepts.name: advantages disadvantages
concepts.description: Strict locking improves consistency but can increase deadlock risk and reduce system throughput.

concepts.name: edge cases examples
concepts.description: A classic example is two transactions each holding one lock and waiting for the other lock.

concepts.name: core definition
concepts.description: Binary search finds a target by repeatedly dividing a sorted search space into halves.

concepts.name: key components
concepts.description: Key components are low index, high index, mid index, and a comparison decision.

concepts.name: working mechanism
concepts.description: If target is less than mid element search left half, otherwise search right half, until found or range is empty.

concepts.name: advantages disadvantages
concepts.description: Binary search is fast at O(log n) but requires sorted data and random access.

concepts.name: edge cases examples
concepts.description: Edge cases include duplicates, missing targets, and integer overflow when calculating midpoint in some languages.

concepts.name: core definition
concepts.description: Recursion is a technique where a function solves a problem by calling itself on smaller subproblems.

concepts.name: key components
concepts.description: A recursive solution needs a base case and a recursive case.

concepts.name: working mechanism
concepts.description: Each call pushes a new frame to the call stack until base case, then unwinds and combines results.

concepts.name: advantages disadvantages
concepts.description: Recursion improves readability for divide-and-conquer problems but may add call overhead and stack overflow risk.

concepts.name: edge cases examples
concepts.description: Examples include factorial, tree traversal, and Fibonacci where missing base case causes infinite recursion.

concepts.name: core definition
concepts.description: Time complexity describes how an algorithm's running time grows with input size using asymptotic notation.

concepts.name: key components
concepts.description: Big-O, Omega, and Theta represent upper bound, lower bound, and tight bound of growth.

concepts.name: working mechanism
concepts.description: Complexity is derived by counting dominant operations and ignoring constants and lower-order terms.

concepts.name: advantages disadvantages
concepts.description: Asymptotic analysis helps compare algorithms but may hide real hardware constants and practical behavior.

concepts.name: edge cases examples
concepts.description: Binary search is O(log n), merge sort is O(n log n), and nested loops are often O(n^2).

concepts.name: core definition
concepts.description: Normalization organizes relational tables to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity.

concepts.name: key components
concepts.description: Normal forms include 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, and BCNF based on dependency rules.

concepts.name: working mechanism
concepts.description: Tables are decomposed so each non-key attribute depends on key, whole key, and nothing but the key.

concepts.name: advantages disadvantages
concepts.description: Normalization reduces anomalies but can increase joins and query complexity.

concepts.name: edge cases examples
concepts.description: Denormalization is sometimes used in analytics systems for faster read performance.

concepts.name: core definition
concepts.description: TCP is connection-oriented and reliable, while UDP is connectionless and best-effort.

concepts.name: key components
concepts.description: TCP uses handshakes, sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmissions; UDP sends datagrams without reliability mechanisms.

concepts.name: working mechanism
concepts.description: TCP ensures ordered delivery and flow control, whereas UDP prioritizes low latency and minimal overhead.

concepts.name: advantages disadvantages
concepts.description: TCP is reliable but heavier; UDP is faster but may lose, reorder, or duplicate packets.

concepts.name: edge cases examples
concepts.description: Web browsing typically uses TCP, while video calls and gaming often use UDP.

concepts.name: core definition
concepts.description: A neural network is a layered function approximator that learns patterns from data using weighted connections.

concepts.name: key components
concepts.description: Main components are input layer, hidden layers, output layer, activation functions, and loss function.

concepts.name: working mechanism
concepts.description: Training uses forward pass, loss computation, backpropagation, and gradient descent updates.

concepts.name: advantages disadvantages
concepts.description: Neural networks capture complex relationships but need large data, compute resources, and careful regularization.

concepts.name: edge cases examples
concepts.description: Overfitting can occur on small datasets; dropout and early stopping are common mitigations.
